items.name: measure_ordinal_type_mismatch
items.msg_str:
Expression is expected to have type `Ordinal.t`,
but its inferred type is `int`.
items.repro_iml:
type action = Identity | Increment | Decrement

let apply_action (act : action) (x : int) : int =
  match act with
  | Identity -> x
  | Increment -> x + 1
  | Decrement -> x - 1

let process_actions_measure (actions : action list) (state : int) : int =
  List.length actions

let rec process_actions (actions : action list) (state : int) : int =
  match actions with
  | [] -> state
  | act :: rest ->
      let new_state = apply_action act state in
      process_actions rest new_state
[@@measure process_actions_measure actions state]

err_msg.msg:
Expression is expected to have type `Ordinal.t`,
but its inferred type is `int`.

items.solution:
type action = Identity | Increment | Decrement

let apply_action (act : action) (x : int) : int =
  match act with
  | Identity -> x
  | Increment -> x + 1
  | Decrement -> x - 1

let process_actions_measure (actions : action list) (state : int) : Ordinal.t =
  Ordinal.of_int (List.length actions)

let rec process_actions (actions : action list) (state : int) : int =
  match actions with
  | [] -> state
  | act :: rest ->
      let new_state = apply_action act state in
      process_actions rest new_state
[@@measure process_actions_measure actions state]
items.explanation:
Termination measures in IML must return `Ordinal.t`, not `int`. The `[@@measure ...]`
attribute expects a function that returns an ordinal value to prove well-founded recursion.
items.solution_description: Wrap the measure function's return value with `Ordinal.of_int` to convert int to Ordinal.t.

items.name: function_list_first_order_violation
items.msg_str:
Function definition is not valid:
Functions must return a first-order type,
but `ls_f` returns `(int -> int) list`
items.repro_iml:
let f (x : int) : int = x + 1

let ls_f = [f; f; f]

err_msg.msg:
Function definition is not valid:
Functions must return a first-order type,
but `ls_f` returns `(int -> int) list`

items.solution:
let f (x : int) : int = x + 1

type action =
  | AddOne
  | AddTwo
  | AddThree

let apply_action (act : action) (x : int) : int =
  match act with
  | AddOne -> x + 1
  | AddTwo -> x + 2
  | AddThree -> x + 3

let ls_actions = [AddOne; AddOne; AddOne]
items.explanation:
IML enforces a first-order type restriction: functions cannot be elements of composite types
(lists, tuples, records, or algebraic data types). This is required for the formal semantics
and automated reasoning capabilities.
items.solution_description: Use an algebraic data type to represent different actions instead of storing functions.

items.name: max_int_not_available
items.repro_iml:
let clamp (x : int) : int =
  if x > max_int then
    max_int
  else
    x
items.solution:
let max_allowed = 1000000

let clamp (x : int) : int =
  if x > max_allowed then
    max_allowed
  else
    x
items.explanation:
IML integers have arbitrary precision, so there is no built-in `max_int` constant.
Unlike OCaml which has bounded integers, IML can represent arbitrarily large integers.
items.solution_description: Use a specific constant for your use case.
items.solution_description_2: Make max_int opaque if it's externally defined.
items.solution_2:
let max_int : int = () [@@opaque]

let clamp (x : int) : int =
  if x > max_int then
    max_int
  else
    x

items.name: let_star_binding_not_in_scope
items.repro_iml:
let process_options (x : int option) (y : int option) : int option =
  let* x_val = x in
  let* y_val = y in
  Some (x_val + y_val)
items.solution:
let process_options (x : int option) (y : int option) : int option =
  let open Option in
  let* x_val = x in
  let* y_val = y in
  Some (x_val + y_val)
items.explanation:
The `let*` binding operator is part of the Option module but not available globally.
It needs to be brought into scope by opening the Option module or using qualified syntax.
items.solution_description: Open the Option module to use let* binding operator.
items.solution_description_3: Use >>= operator with Option module open.
items.solution_3:
let process_options (x : int option) (y : int option) : int option =
  Option.(x >>= fun x_val ->
  y >>= fun y_val ->
  Some (x_val + y_val))

items.name: real_pow_exponent_must_be_int
items.msg_str:
Application failed: expected argument of type `Int.t`
but got (exponent : real)
items.repro_iml:
let calculate (n : int) : real =
  let base = 2.5 in
  let exponent = Real.of_int (n / 41670) in
  Real.pow base exponent

err_msg.msg:
Application failed: expected argument of type `Int.t`
but got (exponent : real)

items.solution:
let calculate (n : int) : real =
  let base = 2.5 in
  let exponent = Real.of_int (n / 41670) in
  Real.pow base (Real.to_int exponent)
items.explanation:
`Real.pow` has signature `real -> int -> real`. The exponent must be an integer, not a real.
This is because IML only supports integer exponentiation for exact arithmetic.
items.solution_description: Convert the exponent back to int using Real.to_int.
items.solution_description_2: Make a custom power function opaque for real exponents.
items.solution_2:
let real_pow : real -> real -> real = () [@@opaque]

let calculate (n : int) : real =
  let base = 2.5 in
  let exponent = Real.of_int (n / 41670) in
  real_pow base exponent

items.name: list_fold_left2_not_available
items.repro_iml:
let sum_pairs (l1 : int list) (l2 : int list) : int =
  List.fold_left2 (fun acc x y -> acc + x + y) 0 l1 l2
items.solution:
let sum_pairs (l1 : int list) (l2 : int list) : int =
  match List.map2 (fun x y -> (x, y)) l1 l2 with
  | Error _ -> 0
  | Ok pairs -> List.fold_left (fun acc (x, y) -> acc + x + y) 0 pairs
items.explanation: `List.fold_left2` does not exist in IML's List module. IML has `List.map2` but not fold_left2.
items.solution_description: Use List.map2 to create pairs first, then fold.
items.solution_2:
let rec sum_pairs (l1 : int list) (l2 : int list) : int =
  match l1, l2 with
  | [], [] -> 0
  | x :: xs, y :: ys -> x + y + sum_pairs xs ys
  | _, _ -> 0

items.name: real_comparison_type_mismatch
items.msg_str:
Application failed: expected argument of type `int`
but got (x : real)
items.repro_iml:
let some_function (x : real) : real =
  if x > max_real then
    max_real
  else
    x

err_msg.msg:
Application failed: expected argument of type `int`
but got (x : real)

items.solution:
let max_real_bound = 1000000.0

let some_function (x : real) : real =
  if x >. max_real_bound then
    max_real_bound
  else
    x
items.explanation:
Two issues: (1) `max_real` is not a built-in constant in IML, and (2) the comparison
operator `>` is for integers. Real comparison requires different operators like `>.`,
`<.`, `>=.`, `<=.` (though the standard operators now work for reals too).
items.solution_description: Define a specific bound and use real comparison operator.
items.solution_description_2: Make max_real opaque and use >= which works for reals.
items.solution_2:
let max_real : real = () [@@opaque]

let some_function (x : real) : real =
  if x >= max_real then
    max_real
  else
    x

items.name: array_not_available
items.solution:
(* Use list instead of Array *)
let example () =
  let arr = List.mapi (fun _ _ -> 0) (0 -- 5) in
  arr
items.explanation:
Arrays are mutable data structures and not available in IML, which is a pure functional
language without mutable state. All operations must be immutable.
items.solution_description: Use immutable lists with functional updates instead of arrays.

items.name: option_map2_not_available
items.repro_iml:
let add_options (x : int option) (y : int option) : int option =
  Option.map2 (fun a b -> a + b) x y
items.solution:
let add_options (x : int option) (y : int option) : int option =
  Option.monoid_product x y |> Option.map (fun (a, b) -> a + b)
items.explanation:
`Option.map2` does not exist in IML's Option module. Use `Option.monoid_product`
or monadic composition instead.
items.solution_2:
let add_options (x : int option) (y : int option) : int option =
  let open Option in
  let+ a = x
  and+ b = y in
  a + b

items.name: min_int_not_available
items.repro_iml:
let clamp (x : int) : int =
  if x < min_int then
    min_int
  else
    x
items.solution:
let min_allowed = -1000000

let clamp (x : int) : int =
  if x < min_allowed then
    min_allowed
  else
    x
items.explanation:
Similar to `max_int`, IML does not have a built-in `min_int` constant because integers
have arbitrary precision and can be arbitrarily small (negative).
items.solution_description_2: Make min_int opaque if it's externally defined.
items.solution_2:
let min_int : int = () [@@opaque]

let clamp (x : int) : int =
  if x < min_int then
    min_int
  else
    x

items.name: option_bind_not_available
items.repro_iml:
let process (x : int option) : int option =
  Option.bind x (fun v -> Some (v + 1))
items.solution:
let process (x : int option) : int option =
  Option.flat_map (fun v -> Some (v + 1)) x
items.explanation:
In IML, the function is called `Option.flat_map` instead of `Option.bind`. The monadic
bind operator is available as `>>=` within the Option module.
items.solution_2:
let process (x : int option) : int option =
  Option.(x >>= fun v -> Some (v + 1))
items.solution_3:
let process (x : int option) : int option =
  let open Option in
  let* v = x in
  Some (v + 1)

items.name: failwith_not_available
items.repro_iml:
let divide (x : int) (y : int) : int =
  if y = 0 then
    failwith "Division by zero"
  else
    x / y
items.solution:
let divide (x : int) (y : int) : int option =
  if y = 0 then
    None
  else
    Some (x / y)
items.explanation:
IML is a pure language without exceptions. The `failwith` function from OCaml is not
available. Error handling must use `Option` or `Result` types instead.
items.solution_description: Return an option type with None for error cases.
items.solution_description_2: Return a Result type with error message.
items.solution_2:
let divide (x : int) (y : int) : (int, string) result =
  if y = 0 then
    Error "Division by zero"
  else
    Ok (x / y)
items.solution_description_3: Return a sentinel value (changes function signature semantics).
items.solution_3:
let divide (x : int) (y : int) : int =
  if y = 0 then
    0
  else
    x / y

items.name: ref_not_available
items.explanation:
References (`ref`) are mutable state and not available in IML, which is purely functional.
All state changes must be explicit through function parameters and return values.
items.solution_description: Use a parameter to track state.
items.solution_2:
let rec count_up_to (n : int) (current : int) : int =
  if current >= n then
    current
  else
    count_up_to n (current + 1)

items.name: monadic_binding_pattern_match
items.explanation:
The `let*` binding operator is not available globally in IML. It must be brought into
scope by opening the appropriate module (Option, Result, etc.).
items.solution_description: Replace monadic binding with explicit pattern matching.

items.name: real_comparison_operators
items.msg_str:
Application failed: expected argument of type `int`
but got (price : real)
items.repro_iml:
let price : real = 10.5
let threshold : real = 5.0
let max_price : real = 100.0

let result =
  if price < threshold then
    Some price
  else if price > max_price then
    Some max_price
  else
    None

err_msg.msg:
Application failed: expected argument of type `int`
but got (price : real)

items.solution:
let price : real = 10.5
let threshold : real = 5.0
let max_price : real = 100.0

let result =
  if Real.(<) price threshold then
    Some price
  else if Real.(>) price max_price then
    Some max_price
  else
    None
items.explanation:
IML's standard comparison operators (<, >, <=, >=) are typed for integers by default.
Using them on real numbers causes a type error. The Real module provides separate
comparison operators for real numbers.
items.solution_description: Use Real module comparison operators: Real.(<), Real.(>), Real.(<=), Real.(>=).

items.name: termination_measure_annotation
items.repro_iml:
let rec left_pad c n xs =
  if List.length xs >= n then
    xs
  else
    left_pad c n (c :: xs)

err_msg.backtrace:
Raised at Stdlib__Hashtbl.MakeSeeded.find in file "hashtbl.ml", line 391, characters 17-32
Called from Imandrax_api__HashtblCache.Make.cached in file "vendor/imandrax-api/src/core/HashtblCache.ml", line 11, characters 10-22

items.solution:
let left_pad_measure n xs =
  Ordinal.of_int (n - List.length xs)

let rec left_pad c n xs =
  if List.length xs >= n then
    xs
  else
    left_pad c n (c :: xs)
[@@measure left_pad_measure n xs]
items.explanation:
IML cannot automatically prove termination when the recursion pattern is non-standard
(e.g., recursion on list length rather than list structure). An explicit measure
annotation is required to show what decreases with each recursive call.
items.solution_description:
Add [@@measure ...] annotation with a function returning Ordinal.t that provably
decreases on each recursive call.

items.name: polymorphic_closure_type_mismatch
items.msg_str:
Application failed: expected argument of type `file_section list`
but got (r.partitions : data_partition list)

err_msg.msg:
Application failed: expected argument of type `file_section list`
but got (r.partitions : data_partition list)

items.explanation:
When List.filter, List.map, or other higher-order List functions are used multiple
times in the same file with lambdas at different types, IML's type inference can fail
to resolve the polymorphic closure type. The lambda parameter gets inferred as the
wrong type from a previous usage of the same higher-order function.
items.solution_description:
Add explicit type annotations to all lambda parameters in higher-order function calls.
For example, change `List.filter (fun x -> x.is_active) items` to
`List.filter (fun (x : item) -> x.is_active) items`.
